quiz_questions.question_text: What is a non-inertial reference frame?

options.text: A reference frame that is accelerating or rotating
A frame at rest
A frame moving at constant velocity
Any frame on Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-inertial frame accelerates or rotates. In such frames, Newton's laws don't hold in their usual form — pseudo-forces must be introduced.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a pseudo-force (fictitious force)?

options.text: An apparent force that appears in a non-inertial reference frame to account for the frame's acceleration — it has no real physical source
A real force that is very weak
A force that only appears in textbooks
A force that only acts on light objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Pseudo-forces arise because the reference frame itself is accelerating. They have no agent (no third law partner) and disappear when viewed from an inertial frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: When you are in a car that accelerates forward, you feel pushed backward. What is this 'force'?

options.text: A pseudo-force — your body's inertia resists the forward acceleration, creating the sensation of being pushed backward in the car's non-inertial frame
A real backward force from the engine
Gravity shifting direction
Air pressure inside the car

quiz_questions.explanation: In the car's (accelerating) frame, a pseudo-force pushes you backward. In an inertial frame view, your body simply maintains its velocity while the car accelerates forward beneath you.

quiz_questions.question_text: The centrifugal force is what type of force?

options.text: A pseudo-force — it appears in rotating reference frames, pointing outward from the rotation axis
A real contact force
A fundamental force of nature
The reaction to centripetal force

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal force is fictitious — it only appears when you analyze motion from a rotating frame. In an inertial frame, objects move in straight lines; no outward force exists.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a rotating reference frame, a ball rolling across a spinning platform curves to one side. What pseudo-force causes this?

options.text: The Coriolis force
Centrifugal force
Gravity
Friction

quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis force is a pseudo-force that deflects moving objects in rotating frames. It is perpendicular to the object's velocity in the rotating frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: Do pseudo-forces obey Newton's third law?

options.text: No — pseudo-forces have no reaction pair because they are not real interactions between objects
Yes — all forces obey Newton's third law
Only in some cases
Yes — the reaction is in the inertial frame

quiz_questions.explanation: Pseudo-forces violate Newton's third law — there is no 'other object' providing the reaction force. This is a key way to identify them as fictitious.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an elevator accelerating downward at g, what apparent weight do you feel?

options.text: Zero — you feel weightless because the pseudo-force in the elevator frame cancels gravity
Double your normal weight
Your normal weight
Half your normal weight

quiz_questions.explanation: In the elevator's frame: F_pseudo = mg upward (opposing the downward acceleration). Net: mg (down) − mg (pseudo up) = 0. You feel weightless.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a spinning merry-go-round, which way does the centrifugal pseudo-force point?

options.text: Outward — away from the center of rotation
Inward — toward the center
Tangent to the circle
Downward

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal force points radially outward in the rotating frame. It is the pseudo-force equivalent of the inertia that makes you feel pushed outward on spinning rides.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do we call pseudo-forces 'fictitious'?

options.text: Because they have no real physical origin — they are mathematical corrections needed to make Newton's laws work in non-inertial frames
Because they cannot be measured
Because they are very small
Because they only exist in theory

quiz_questions.explanation: Pseudo-forces are called fictitious because they don't arise from real interactions between objects. They are artifacts of using an accelerating reference frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Coriolis effect on Earth causes large-scale wind patterns to curve. In the Northern Hemisphere, moving air deflects to the:

options.text: Right
Left
Straight ahead
Upward

quiz_questions.explanation: In the Northern Hemisphere, the Coriolis effect deflects moving objects to the right of their direction of travel. This causes cyclones to spin counterclockwise.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a non-inertial reference frame?

options.text: A reference frame that is accelerating, decelerating, or rotating, in which Newton's laws of motion do not hold in their standard form without introducing fictitious forces
A frame that moves at constant velocity
A frame attached to a very large object
A frame where no experiments can be performed

quiz_questions.explanation: In a non-inertial frame, objects appear to accelerate without any apparent real force. To 'fix' Newton's second law in such frames, pseudo-forces are added.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a pseudo-force?

options.text: An apparent force introduced in a non-inertial reference frame to make Newton's second law valid — it equals −ma_frame where a_frame is the frame's acceleration
A very weak real force
A force that only acts on imaginary objects
Any force that is difficult to measure

quiz_questions.explanation: Pseudo-force = −m × a_frame. It has no physical agent, no third law pair, and vanishes when viewed from an inertial frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes centrifugal force?

options.text: The pseudo-force that appears in a rotating reference frame, directed radially outward from the axis of rotation, equal to mω²r
A real force pushing objects outward in circular motion
The same as centripetal force
A force that only works in space

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal force (mω²r, outward) is the pseudo-force in a rotating frame. It is the reason you feel pushed against the car door in a turn — in the car's rotating frame, this pseudo-force acts outward.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the Coriolis force?

options.text: A pseudo-force in a rotating frame that acts perpendicular to the velocity of a moving object, deflecting its path — equal to −2m(ω × v) in the rotating frame
A force that causes objects to spin faster
The gravitational force on spinning objects
A force that only exists at the equator

quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis force deflects moving objects in rotating frames. On Earth, it affects weather patterns (cyclones), ocean currents, and long-range ballistics. F_Cor = −2m(ω × v).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the equivalence principle?

options.text: Einstein's principle that locally, gravitational effects are indistinguishable from effects of acceleration — a person in an accelerating elevator cannot distinguish the 'force' from real gravity
The principle that all pseudo-forces are equal
The principle that mass equals weight
The principle that all reference frames are equivalent for all experiments

quiz_questions.explanation: The equivalence principle states that a uniform gravitational field is locally indistinguishable from uniform acceleration. This is the foundation of general relativity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes apparent weight?

options.text: The normal force experienced by a person or object, which can differ from true weight in an accelerating (non-inertial) reference frame
Always equal to mg
The mass of an object
Weight measured on a different planet

quiz_questions.explanation: Apparent weight = N (scale reading). In an elevator accelerating up: N = m(g + a) > mg. Accelerating down: N = m(g − a) < mg. Free fall: N = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the Eötvös effect?

options.text: The apparent change in gravitational acceleration experienced by objects moving east or west on a rotating planet, caused by the Coriolis force having a vertical component
The bending of light by gravity
The wobble of Earth's axis
The stretching of objects by tidal forces

quiz_questions.explanation: Moving east on Earth increases your effective orbital speed, producing an outward Coriolis component that slightly reduces apparent gravity. Moving west has the opposite effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes frame-dependent quantities?

options.text: Physical quantities whose measured values depend on the reference frame of the observer — such as velocity, kinetic energy, and momentum
Quantities that are the same in all frames
Quantities that can only be measured in one frame
Quantities that have no units

quiz_questions.explanation: Many quantities are frame-dependent: velocity, KE, momentum. However, some quantities are frame-independent (invariant): mass, charge, spacetime interval (in relativity).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes tidal force in the context of non-inertial frames?

options.text: The differential force arising because a gravitational field varies in strength and direction across an extended body, causing stretching along the direction toward the source
The force of ocean tides on a ship
The centrifugal force on the Moon
A pseudo-force that exists only in rotating frames

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal forces arise from gravitational gradients. Near Earth's center, gravity partially cancels, but the near and far sides experience different pulls, stretching the body radially.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the rotating frame pseudo-force equation?

options.text: F_eff = F_real − mA − 2m(ω × v') − mω × (ω × r'), combining the translational pseudo-force, Coriolis force, and centrifugal force terms
F = ma in an inertial frame
F = mv²/r for circular motion
F = −kx for springs

quiz_questions.explanation: In a general non-inertial (rotating + translating) frame: F_eff = F_real + F_translational + F_Coriolis + F_centrifugal. This allows Newton's second law to be applied in the non-inertial frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: You stand on a scale in an elevator accelerating upward at 3 m/s². Your mass is 70 kg. What does the scale read? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 910 N — apparent weight = m(g + a) = 70 × 13 = 910 N

quiz_questions.explanation: Apparent weight = N = m(g + a) = 70(10 + 3) = 910 N. The upward acceleration adds to the gravitational effect, making you feel heavier.

quiz_questions.question_text: You are in a van that brakes suddenly. A ball on the dashboard rolls forward. Explain from both inertial and non-inertial perspectives.

options.text: Inertial: The ball continues at its original velocity (inertia) while the van decelerates beneath it; Non-inertial: A pseudo-force pushes the ball forward in the decelerating van's frame
Both frames see the ball pushed by the brake force
The ball rolls due to gravity in both frames
Only the inertial perspective is correct

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertial frame: ball maintains its velocity (Newton's first law); van slows. Non-inertial (van) frame: ball accelerates forward due to a pseudo-force F = −ma_van. Both descriptions are self-consistent.

quiz_questions.question_text: A plumb bob hangs in an accelerating train. The train accelerates forward at 5 m/s². At what angle does the bob hang from vertical? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: arctan(5/10) = arctan(0.5) ≈ 26.6° from vertical, angled backward

quiz_questions.explanation: In the train's frame, pseudo-force = ma backward and gravity = mg downward. The bob aligns with the resultant: tanθ = a/g = 5/10 = 0.5 → θ ≈ 26.6° from vertical.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a rotating space station designed to simulate gravity, the 'floor' is the outer wall. If the station has radius 100 m and rotates to produce 1g of artificial gravity, what is the rotation rate?

options.text: ω = √(g/r) = √(9.8/100) ≈ 0.313 rad/s, or about 3 RPM
1 RPM
10 RPM
0.1 rad/s

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal acceleration = ω²r = g → ω = √(g/r) = √(9.8/100) ≈ 0.313 rad/s = 0.313 × 60/(2π) ≈ 3 RPM.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person in a car feels pushed to the right when the car turns left. Explain this using pseudo-forces.

options.text: In the car's non-inertial (turning) frame, a centrifugal pseudo-force pushes the person outward (to the right) — in an inertial frame, the person's body simply continues straight while the car turns left
A real force from the seat pushes the person right
Gravity shifts direction during turns
The door pushes the person inward

quiz_questions.explanation: From inside the turning car (non-inertial frame): a centrifugal pseudo-force acts outward (rightward). From outside (inertial frame): the person's inertia carries them straight while the car curves left.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Coriolis effect deflects a ball thrown northward on a rotating turntable (counterclockwise as seen from above). Which way does the ball deflect?

options.text: To the right (east) — in a counterclockwise rotating frame, the Coriolis force deflects motion to the right
To the left (west)
Straight up
It doesn't deflect

quiz_questions.explanation: For counterclockwise rotation, F_Cor = −2m(ω × v) deflects objects to the right of their velocity direction. This is analogous to the Northern Hemisphere's Coriolis effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronaut in a freely falling spacecraft releases a ball. The ball appears to float motionless beside her. Explain using pseudo-forces.

options.text: In the spacecraft's non-inertial frame (free-fall), the gravitational pseudo-force exactly cancels real gravity, making the effective gravity zero — everything inside appears weightless
The ball has no mass in space
There is no gravity in space
The ball is held by air pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: In the free-falling frame, the pseudo-force is mg upward, exactly canceling the real gravitational force mg downward. The net effective force is zero, producing apparent weightlessness.

quiz_questions.question_text: Earth rotates. At the equator, how does this affect your apparent weight compared to the poles?

options.text: Your apparent weight is slightly less at the equator because the centrifugal pseudo-force (pointing outward) partially opposes gravity — the effect is about 0.3%
Apparent weight is greater at the equator
There is no difference
Apparent weight is zero at the equator

quiz_questions.explanation: At the equator, centrifugal acceleration = ω²R ≈ 0.034 m/s² outward. This reduces apparent g by about 0.3%. At the poles, there is no centrifugal effect (r = 0).

quiz_questions.question_text: A helicopter drops supplies to a ship. If the ship is at latitude 60°N and the supplies fall for 10 seconds, roughly which direction do they deflect due to the Coriolis effect?

options.text: The supplies deflect to the east — falling objects in the Northern Hemisphere are deflected eastward by the Coriolis effect because they move from a region of lower to higher angular velocity
They deflect to the west
They deflect to the north
There is no deflection

quiz_questions.explanation: A falling object at 60°N moves from a higher altitude (slower Earth rotation speed) to a lower altitude (faster rotation at the ground). In the Earth's frame, this appears as an eastward deflection.

quiz_questions.question_text: A double-decker bus rounds a curve. Passengers on the upper deck feel more 'pushed outward' than those on the lower deck. Why?

options.text: The upper deck is farther from the road surface and closer to the center of the curve doesn't explain it. Actually, passengers on the upper deck are at a greater height, and the bus tilts slightly, creating a larger effective pseudo-force displacement. More precisely, at greater height the tangential speed is slightly higher during body sway, amplifying the sensation

The upper deck passengers experience greater centrifugal pseudo-force because they are farther from the center of the bus's turn radius (the center of the road curve) — since centrifugal force = mω²r, a larger effective r gives a larger force. Also, the upper deck sways more
The upper deck has less friction
Gravity is stronger at greater heights

quiz_questions.explanation: At the upper deck, passengers are farther from the center of the curve (larger effective radius), so the centrifugal pseudo-force (mω²r) is slightly larger. Additionally, the greater height amplifies the tipping sensation.
